APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses neural relation detection for KBQA—a core NLP task—using a hierarchical BiLSTM with residual learning and integrates this into an end-to-end KBQA pipeline, which fits top-tier NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP, FB2M), compares to appropriate baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention), and uses accepted metrics (accuracy/top-1), with hyperparameters and related work reported. The problem formulation, methods, and evaluation follow community standards and are targeted to the right audience. | B(5): This work addresses a core NLP/ML topic—relation detection for knowledge-base question answering—and proposes an HR-BiLSTM model with hierarchical matching and residual learning, which is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience by evaluating on standard KBQA benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP, FB2M), comparing to established baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention), and reporting standard metrics (top-1 accuracy). The problem formulation, methods (hierarchical RNNs, residual connections), and evaluation protocol match expectations for this research community and the paper is positioned appropriately for a leading conference.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from method (HR-BiLSTM and hierarchical matching with residual learning) to experiments and conclusions, and the main contributions (improved relation detector and KBQA integration) are stated early. Notation and methodological details seem clearly presented: hierarchical/relation representations, datasets, baselines, metrics, and hyperparameters are reported. Experimental reporting is thorough (datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics) and the writing quality is described as good, suggesting only minor presentation issues remain. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from introduction through method (HR-BiLSTM and residual learning) to experiments (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) and conclusion, and its main contributions are stated early (improved relation detector and KBQA integration). Notation and experimental details seem adequately reported (hyperparameters given, datasets and baselines clearly listed), and related work coverage is thorough. The only minor presentation gaps from the summary are an absence of explicit comments about figure/table effectiveness and no mention of code availability; overall the writing quality is reported as good.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims (HR-BiLSTM with hierarchical matching and residual learning, plus an integrated KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking and two-step relation detection) are clearly stated and appear supported by experimental evidence showing state-of-the-art results on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP against reasonable baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention). Mathematical content is architectural rather than theoretical and no incorrect derivations are indicated; hyperparameters are reported and standard datasets (FB2M subset) are used. Minor issues that prevent a perfect score are the lack of mention of statistical significance testing and no code release noted in the summary, which limits reproducibility and confidence in marginal improvements. | B(4): The main claims (HR-BiLSTM with residual learning and a KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking/two-step relation detection) are clearly stated and supported by experimental results reporting state-of-the-art accuracy on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP against appropriate baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention). There are no mathematical proofs to verify; model design and hyperparameters are reported. The experimental methodology is largely sound (reasonable baselines, two datasets, hyperparameters reported), but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, and the authors acknowledge potential improvements (e.g., attention for long sequences), which are minor limitations. Overall the results support the conclusions, with only modest missing details that do not undermine the main technical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims (HR-BiLSTM with hierarchical matching and residual learning, plus an integrated KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking and two-step relation detection) are clearly stated and appear supported by experimental evidence showing state-of-the-art results on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP against reasonable baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention). Mathematical content is architectural rather than theoretical and no incorrect derivations are indicated; hyperparameters are reported and standard datasets (FB2M subset) are used. Minor issues that prevent a perfect score are the lack of mention of statistical significance testing and no code release noted in the summary, which limits reproducibility and confidence in marginal improvements.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (HR-BiLSTM with residual learning and a KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking/two-step relation detection) are clearly stated and supported by experimental results reporting state-of-the-art accuracy on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP against appropriate baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention). There are no mathematical proofs to verify; model design and hyperparameters are reported. The experimental methodology is largely sound (reasonable baselines, two datasets, hyperparameters reported), but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, and the authors acknowledge potential improvements (e.g., attention for long sequences), which are minor limitations. Overall the results support the conclusions, with only modest missing details that do not undermine the main technical claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Experiments cover both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) settings, compare to several strong baselines, and claim state-of-the-art results, which supports the main claims. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses for components like the hierarchical matching and residual connections, a notable gap. Related work is reported as thorough, and the paper discusses limitations (e.g., attention as future work and data sparsity issues) and reports hyperparameters, aiding reproducibility even though code availability is not mentioned. | B(4): Experiments are reasonably comprehensive: the paper evaluates the proposed HR-BiLSTM relation detector and the integrated KBQA pipeline against strong baselines on both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) datasets and reports state-of-the-art results. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not mentioned in the summary (no explicit ablation of hierarchical levels or residual connections), though hyperparameters are reported. Related work coverage is described as thorough and some limitations (e.g., future attention-based matching, dataset challenges) are discussed. Reproducibility is supported by dataset/method detail and hyperparameter reporting, but lack of code is not mentioned, so minor gaps remain.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the core KBQA problem of relation detection and proposes a concrete architectural advance (HR-BiLSTM with hierarchical matching and residual learning) that is directly applicable to QA pipelines. It demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) benchmarks and integrates entity re-ranking and a two-step relation detection that improve end-to-end QA, which makes the approach practically relevant. These results are likely to influence follow-up work on relation representation and KBQA system design, opening clear directions for hierarchical matching in QA, though the contribution is evolutionary rather than transformational. | B(4): The paper tackles the central problem of relation detection for KBQA and proposes a concrete architectural improvement (HR-BiLSTM with hierarchical question–relation matching and residual learning) that is shown to produce state-of-the-art accuracy on both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) benchmarks. By integrating the improved detector into a practical KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking and two-step relation detection, the work demonstrates clear utility for end-to-end systems and is likely to influence how relation detection is engineered in future KBQA research. While impactful within the subfield and likely to be widely adopted, the contribution appears evolutionary rather than broadly transformative across NLP, and some extensions (e.g., hierarchical attention for long sequences) remain for future work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the core KBQA problem of relation detection and proposes a concrete architectural advance (HR-BiLSTM with hierarchical matching and residual learning) that is directly applicable to QA pipelines. It demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) benchmarks and integrates entity re-ranking and a two-step relation detection that improve end-to-end QA, which makes the approach practically relevant. These results are likely to influence follow-up work on relation representation and KBQA system design, opening clear directions for hierarchical matching in QA, though the contribution is evolutionary rather than transformational.

By integrating the improved detector into a practical KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking and two-step relation detection, the work demonstrates clear utility for end-to-end systems and is likely to influence how relation detection is engineered in future KBQA research. While impactful within the subfield and likely to be widely adopted, the contribution appears evolutionary rather than broadly transformative across NLP, and some extensions (e.g., hierarchical attention for long sequences) remain for future work.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes HR-BiLSTM (hierarchical matching between questions and relations with residual/shortcut connections) and a KBQA pipeline that adds entity re-ranking and two-step relation detection. These are clear, practical extensions over prior relation detectors (e.g., STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention) and the authors explicitly differentiate their contributions. However, the core ideas (hierarchical representations, BiLSTMs, residual connections, and multi-step reranking) are combinations and adaptations of existing techniques rather than wholly novel paradigms, so the contribution is incremental with moderate novelty. | B(3): The paper proposes a focused architectural innovation (HR-BiLSTM) that applies hierarchical question–relation matching with residual (shortcut) connections and uses this within a KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking and two-step relation detection. These ideas appear to be task-specific combinations and refinements of existing techniques (hierarchical representation, residual connections, and pipeline re-ranking) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, and the authors compare against established baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention). Given the novelty is primarily an incremental but well-differentiated model and engineering of the KBQA pipeline that yields SOTA on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP, the contribution is moderately novel rather than highly original.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes HR-BiLSTM (hierarchical matching between questions and relations with residual/shortcut connections) and a KBQA pipeline that adds entity re-ranking and two-step relation detection. These are clear, practical extensions over prior relation detectors (e.g., STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention) and the authors explicitly differentiate their contributions. However, the core ideas (hierarchical representations, BiLSTMs, residual connections, and multi-step reranking) are combinations and adaptations of existing techniques rather than wholly novel paradigms, so the contribution is incremental with moderate novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a focused architectural innovation (HR-BiLSTM) that applies hierarchical question–relation matching with residual (shortcut) connections and uses this within a KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking and two-step relation detection. These ideas appear to be task-specific combinations and refinements of existing techniques (hierarchical representation, residual connections, and pipeline re-ranking) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, and the authors compare against established baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable attention). Given the novelty is primarily an incremental but well-differentiated model and engineering of the KBQA pipeline that yields SOTA on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP, the contribution is moderately novel rather than highly original.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable-attention variants) on standard datasets (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using the same accuracy/top-1 metrics, and reports hyperparameters, suggesting fair experimental conditions. The manuscript clearly contrasts its hierarchical residual BiLSTM approach with prior models and provides thorough related-work coverage. It is not explicit in the summary whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there may be a minor omission in discussion of when baselines match the proposed approach. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (STAGG 2015; AMPCNN 2016; BiCNN; a decomposable-attention variant) on the standard datasets used (SimpleQuestions and WebQSP) using the same accuracy/top-1 metrics, so comparisons appear fair. The authors also provide a thorough related-work discussion and analyze how their HR-BiLSTM (hierarchical matching with residual learning) differs from prior models and integrate it into a KBQA pipeline. One minor omission is that the summary does not make clear whether the paper explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, though limitations and dataset challenges are discussed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, decomposable-attention variants) on standard datasets (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using the same accuracy/top-1 metrics, and reports hyperparameters, suggesting fair experimental conditions. The manuscript clearly contrasts its hierarchical residual BiLSTM approach with prior models and provides thorough related-work coverage. It is not explicit in the summary whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there may be a minor omission in discussion of when baselines match the proposed approach.

main_claims: An improved relation detection model (HR-BiLSTM) using hierarchical matching between questions and relations with residual learning.
A simple KBQA system that integrates entity linking with the improved relation detector (including entity re-ranking and two-step relation detection) to improve end-to-end QA.
Demonstration that the improved relation detector enables the KBQA system to achieve state-of-the-art results on both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) tasks.
_summary.methodology: They propose a hierarchical recurrent neural network (HR-BiLSTM) with residual (shortcut) connections to learn multi-level question representations and match them to relation representations (word-level and relation-level); this relation detector is integrated into a KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking and a two-step relation detection process.
limitations: The authors note that hierarchical matching with an attention mechanism may work better for long sequences and leave this for future work.
Dataset difficulty: SimpleQuestions has 14% of golden test relations not observed in golden training tuples (noted as a challenge).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Knowledge Base Question Answering (relation detection)
